summary: Atwood uses a psychology experiment about rats crossing electrified grids to reach food as a metaphor for software usability. The 'Columbia Obstruction Device' showed that rats would endure more shocks for better rewards (cheese). He argues that most business software will never be compelling enough ('have enough cheese') to overcome poor usability. Since developers can't make document management systems as desirable as Napster or free music, they must focus on minimizing friction instead. The post concludes that for typical applications, reducing usability barriers is the only viable path to adoption.
key_points: Applications succeed either by being extremely compelling (cheese) or extremely easy to use (low shock) - most can only control the latter
Napster and ICQ proved that truly compelling products can succeed despite terrible interfaces
Most business software will never be inherently desirable enough to overcome usability friction
Usability is often perceived as optional, and for genuinely compelling products it sometimes is
The barrier to entry must be absurdly low for users to even consider trying typical software
money_quotes: Unless you are providing users free mp3s, or access to pornography, it's highly unlikely you will ever have enough cheese to overcome even the mildest of electric shocks.
The barrier to entry has to be absurdly low to even get people to look at your software – much less use it.
An application that does something really great that people really want to do can be pathetically unusable, and it will still be a hit.
Delicious cheese is a rare luxury that most developers working on typical business applications will never have.
What kind of crazy user looks forward to using a document management system?
Forget about the cheese: just make sure you aren't shocking your users.
key_insight: Most software isn't compelling enough to overcome usability friction, so developers should abandon dreams of making their apps desirable and focus entirely on removing barriers to use.
